# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Movies that are visually psychedelic and feel like a fever dream.

**Score:** 37
**Body:** I'm looking for loose, tipsy 2a.m. kind of movies. A tiny bit unsettling but in an arty sort of a way. A whimsy weird dreamy movie. Some movies that I really like were:

1. Mullhound Drive

2. 2001: Space Odyssey

3. Enter into the Void

Basically movies that make me feel high.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas
  - Inherent Vice (2014)
2. A Scanner Darkly

trippy AF

it is equally

fantastic
disturbing

I do not recommend viewing it on shrooms or acid

its trippy enough and hard to follow ... even sober
3. Mandy

Brazil

A Clockwork Orange

Naked Lunch
4. Angel heart
  - twisted it is
5. Koyanaskasi (SP)

Baraka
6. Paprika! Fever dream and a blast
7. The Holy Mountain. I watched it late one night out of curiosity and the whole thing felt like a surreal fever dream from start to finish. Every scene looks like something you’d see in a weird art gallery.
8. Tree of Life

## 2. Pumpkin (2002): The most bizarre film I've ever seen

**Score:** 23
**Body:** My best friend and I sometimes play a game called tubi roulette. There's so much whack shit on there; things so bad you have to laugh, things that are just plain bad, and every now and then you hit gold.

That's how I feel about pumpkin. I'd call it a black comedy, but it's so much more than that. It's satirical without being satire, politically incorrect yet oddly compelling, and just pure campy fun. The cast is stacked with incredible actors before they got big: cristina ricci, melissa mccarhty, and Amy Adams, to name a few. It has the uncanny effect of seeming like a made-for-TV movie while also being kind of arthouse. 

Here's the premise: Carolyn’s sorority sisters set their sights on the Sorority of the Year award and coaching challenged athletes is their ticket to the trophy. But when the queen of formals finds herself mentoring Pumpkin, a disabled athlete, their two worlds collide.

I went into it expecting a rom com, so maybe that's why i was so shocked watching it, but i'd like to hear how other people feel

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Oh my God. Just looked up the trailer on YouTube. I watched it 3 times in a row
2. Soundtrack kicks ass too
  - Yeah, I think this is what got me into Emiliana Torrini!
3. Rented this many years ago. 


My brother-in-law is a huge Christina Ricci fan and had a signed copy, that he lost in a move. 
4. Christina Ricci was well known in 2002
5. I love this movie ! Me and my ex watched it several times back in the day ha ha .
6. Pumpkin and Penelope from ‘06 are rom-coms for salty couples who like a bit of darkness.
7. I’ve seen that movie & really enjoyed it.  Non typical storyline.
8. I love Christina Ricci and I love this movie
9. Just put it on.  Thanks!

## 3. What movie made you rethink the entire story after it ended?

**Score:** 20
**Body:** I’m building a Letterboxd list called “**Films That Make You Rethink Everything.**”

Looking for movies where the ending changes how you see the entire film — twists, unreliable narratives, psychological reveals, anything like that.

Drop your suggestions.

*The most upvoted ones will be added to the list and credited with your Reddit username.*

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Donnie Darko 
2. Haven’t seen anyone mention Arrival, so there’s my contribution. 
3. Shutter island
4. Primer. 
5. Mulholland Dr.
6. The sixth sense 
  - Turns out. Bruce Willis.....was wearing a hair piece the whole time.
7. The Others 
8. Memento
9. The Prestige 

The Machinist

Magnolia 

## 4. Weird/uncomfortable horror adjacent movies

**Score:** 16
**Body:** I just watched midsommar and it was one of the most amazing movies I've ever seen. I don't like most horror, I don't like being super scared, but I loved just like the weird, off puttingness of midsommar. I also love the little nightmares games for that same reason if you know those. What are some good movies to scratch that itch midsommar created?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Wicker Man (1973)

Get Out (2017)
2. **The Lighthouse** is exactly what I think of with this label. I personally liked it much more than Midsommar though. It's more fast paced but even weirder. 
3. The Witch 
4. It Follows 
5. Mother!
6. A Clockwork Orange
7. Super Dark Times (2017)

Don’t Worry Darling (2022) also with Florence Pugh! 
8. I Saw the TV Glow (2024)
9. Existenz 
10. The Lobster would probably fit for this.

## 5. Movies like 10 Cloverfield Lane

**Score:** 14
**Body:** Really enjoyed that movie. Being stuck in a confined place with a madman.. so guess thrillers like that if there are any with a similar vibe. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Ex Machina, Cube and Knock at the Cabin 
2. Bugonia is by far the very closest thing. Great movie
  - That was the craziest shit ever I loved it The whole idea the whole concept was so rad!
3. Check out Dead Calm! A husband, wife, and a killer on a tiny boat in the middle of the ocean.
4. Creep (2014) seems to fit the bill 
5. The Divide. 

6. Funny I was just comparing that movie to Take Shelter (2011)
7. The Perfect Host
  - Came here to say this 
8. Buried with Ryan Reynolds was amazing.

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Most iconic character entrances?

**Score:** 3379

  - ![gif](giphy|14polEn4dwugZa)
3. https://preview.redd.it/t2zvm0y355ng1.png?width=318&format=png&auto=webp&s=405109f0c3f26546e8f1f7519755c1ef0494523f

## 2. Why is no one talking about Regina Hall’s performance in One Battle After Another?

**Score:** 392
**Body:** She was exceptional imo. Personally, better than Teyana Taylor. Regina should have gotten the best supporting actress nomination instead of her imo especially considering Taylor was only in the first 20 minutes of the movie. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Idk man, watched it a few weeks ago,and gonna be hobest i had already forgotten she was in it
  - I’m with you. She was barely in it, and made zero impact on me. 
    - Yeah, shes a fine actress. But there was very little for her to do in the film other than “look concerned”.  
2. Cause we waiting for scary movie
3. Regina Hall was in only 10 mins of the movie is probably the main reason 
4. Expected more battles. 
5. Her character was kinda underwritten. Not enough given to her to do.
6. She's great but there are probably 5 better performances in this movie 
7. Personally, I think she should’ve had more screen time and her character was actually far more interesting than Taylor’s as a whole. Especially when you get into her counterpart from Vineland being one of the most integral characters of that novel. 
8. I don't get this, she is barely in the movie. She has more screen time in the scary movie 6 trailer lol

## 3. Thoughts on Insomnia (2002)? One of my favourite crime thriller movies. It's a very underrated Christopher Nolan movie.

**Score:** 272

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. it's one of my favorite Robin Williams performances
  - My personal favorite Nolan movie. 
2. Excellent film that shows Robin Williams’ (rare) serious side. 
  - He had a little streak of darker movies for a minute there. One Hour Photo, Insomnia, The Final Cut. All superb 
    - Dont forget Death to Smoochy
3. Seeing the original first (and loving it), the Nolan version was underwhelming on my first viewing.

Years later, I rewatched the Nolan version and appreciated it much more removed from the comparison of the original.

Solid film from a director I think is widely overrated.
  - May have to give it another look because the original film, I felt, was leagues better than Nolan’s (and, no, I’m not a Nolan hater but I do feel of his films this may well be his weakest… if only because it so pales versus the original).
4. I love this movie. I’m surprised the studio was so “hands off” with this. That’s just a pure guess on my part….but this movie really seems like the studio didn’t tinker with it at all. 

Some people say Pacino’s larger than life style doesn’t fit the film because it’s a stoic and sort of “minimalist” film. I actually like it, because the protagonist is just completely fucking losing it. His character seems completely lucid and not schizophrenic….but something about him seems off. I love the truck hallucination scene. 
5. I liked it a lot.

The ending was perfect. 

"Let Me Sleep" by David Julyan is a masterpiece.
6. Some movies never get the recognition they deserve and this is one of them imo. Another is Locke.

## 4. "Is This Thing On?" is an Overrated, Pretentious, Waste of Time

**Score:** 116
**Body:** "Is This Thing On?" 

An appropriate title, as it's how Bradley Cooper should feel after making us sit through this.

I don't understand how this film got such acclaim. Nothing happens in the movie. I don't mean that in a fun, Seinfeld, "film about nothing" way. I mean literally, nothing happens.

The premise is, a man gets divorced and finds therapy in doing stand-up comedy. There is no plot, really. Our protagonist undergoes no discernable arc, the couple is never really technically divorced, the little conflict that does happen is trivial and pointless, failing to provide us with a plot or give the film any forward motion. 

The movie goes from divorce scenes, to scenes where Will Arnett bombs at the Comedy Cellar. His awkward five minute sets, of which we must sit through their entirety, seem to last about 10 minutes.

Then, cut back to divorce and arguing. Maybe he interacts with his kids, maybe he interacts with the wife, or his parents, or his wife interacts with friends etc. I just refer to these scenes as "divorce scenes." After the divorce scenes we go back to the Comedy Cellar.

That's the entire movie. Just back and forth between Will Arnett bombing at the Cellar, and arguing with his wife.

The entire third act takes place in an Air BnB, where the couple sometimes gets along and sometimes argues. It is at this point you begin to miss his bad comedy set, because literally nothing is happening.

Bradley Cooper presumptuously inserts himself into the film as...somebody. I really didn't understand his character. Sometimes he was wacky, sometimes he was serious, I'm not really sure what he was going for.

There are scenes in this film that last so long, I swear Laura Dern was searching for lines. How much did they pay her to be in this? Surely she didn't read the script and say, "Wow, this is exciting!" This was a favor or something. I mean, she was great with the crap script she was given.

One of the characters is a cameo, a singer or something. Cooper decides to dedicate about 6 minutes of screen time to her singing ability, for no reason at all. It has nothing to do with the rest of the movie. Well, I say that as if the rest of the movie had any point anyway.

I can't believe this got great reviews. Viewing this is a slogfest. I sat through this whole thing, expecting some big twist or some kind of conflict to arise. Nothing happens in the movie.

I repeat.

Nothing. Happens. In. This. Movie.





### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Should the guy in the $3000 suit be called pretentious by someone who doesn’t make that in a month? Come on!
2. Idk my wife and I were crying the whole way thru. The themes of depression and not being alive really resonated with me. Very strong movie.
  - https://preview.redd.it/jjxi4jua3bng1.jpeg?width=640&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=c57f21f85c43bae2041b986bc73531a2f848d275

I’m not doing Will Arnett and depression again. Not after last time.
3. I thought Arnett was terrific in this movie!
  - I prefer him as a magician.
    - Illusionist, Michael!!!
      - Tricks are something whores do
      - ![gif](giphy|XnpCSHmqeMWJi)
        - Later that afternoon: 

I don’t care for Gob.
    - *I’ve made a huuuge mistake*

## 5. What’s a movie you thought was good but only needed to see once because it was a lot

**Score:** 108

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Grave of the Fireflies.  Best movie I never want to see again. 
2. Requiem is the obvious go to, but I’ll say Life Is Beautiful 
3. Can we go one day without a Requiem for a Dream post? 
4. Out of these, Kids is the top for me. 

Bully and Funny Games would be others. 
5. Uncut Gems. Good Time. Safdie brothers sure do have a style haha
6. Sleepers 
7. Mystic River
8. Million Dollar Baby
9. Grave of the Fireflies for sure.
10. Blue Valentine with Ryan Gosling. HEAVY.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. What are some great films that have bad or mediocre scripts?

**Score:** 35
**Body:** The other day I was talking to a friend over a movie we both watched, the conversation shifted to screenwriting  and off-handedly they said something along the lines of  “a movie can still be great even if it has a shit screenplay, what really counts if the director’s good enough” and that sentence has been on my mind a lot lately, mostly because of how silly I think it is, obviously the second half of the statement is true, a great cast, crew and director are integral to making a great movie, and them translating the script to the screen during the filmmaking proccess matters more but the script has to be at least competent enough for the filmmaker to create a great movie no? I cant think of a movie that had a truly awful script but still came out great (The Departed maybe?) if anything the opposite is true, there’s a lot of films directed by hacks or mid-tier directors that still end up being good because the script was really great.

I’d love to be proven wrong though so that’s why I wanted to ask if anyone here knows any movies that had a bad/mid script that still ended up being good? (goes without saying but “so bad it’s good” movies dont count)

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I heard Colin Quinn once say that the script for Ace Ventura had been floating around Hollywood for years and was terrible - Jim Carrey's performance and improv while shooting singlehandedly turned it into a hit.
  - Another good answer. Everything redeemable about it is due to Jim Carrey going full Jim Carrey.
2. "With a good script, a good director can produce a masterpiece. With the same script, a mediocre director can produce a passable film. But with a bad script, even a good director can't possibly make a good film." - Akira Kurosawa
3. I wouldn't call it a bad script, but Days of Heaven went through so much work in the editing process that the script kind of feels like a complete afterthought next to the visuals. The characters barely speak and don't have a lot of depth, but it doesn't matter because the sheer sense of place is overwhelming.
4. The movie that comes immediately to mind for me is Suspiria. Where would we be without Suspiria? The movie invented about eight new colors. But the script is bad. Not just “formulaic” — I disagree with the other poster who said Star Wars has a bad script; Star Wars has a very good script — but bad: clunky dialogue, very little narrative momentum, nothing going on in its head. Doesn’t matter at all. It’s unclear whether a better script would make the movie any better.
  - I need to step in re. Suspiria because I think its script fits it perfectly - everything in the movie is off-kilter or uncanny, and the script follows suit. Deep Red I’d say is a better example because for the most part that’s a more ‘natural’ movie where the clunky dialogue can feel at odds with the rest of the experience. But Suspiria with ‘good’ dialogue would be all kinds of wrong, I think. I’m not entirely convinced that was the intent behind the writing, but it all gels so I try not to worry about it.
5. I don't have a lot of specific answers for this question, but I'm curious to get more clarity on what a "bad script" is. because a script can be bad in so many ways! is it bad dialogue? bad plotting? bad pacing? where does "bad story" end and "bad script" begin? I have some ideas, but i think drilling down more and getting more clarity on these points will help identify possible candidates
  - Usually people mean very basic and cliche, like Drive.

It could also mean repetitive and over-plotted-to-the-point-of-confusing, like Transformers III or Pirates of the Caribbean II
6. There seem to be a lot of gripes about some great films with pretty simple stories, which I think is only fair to a point. For starters, unless you’ve actually read the scripts, it’s not fair to rag on it just based on seeing the movie. For example, Mark Smith’s script for REVENANT and Hoseein Amini’s for DRIVE are both very good scripts, if perhaps not groundbreaking stories,and both were elevated by directing. 

The other category, of the Argento or Bava or even John Carpenter camps, you could maybe argue weren’t great but they were tailored to what those directors wanted from cinema. 

As far as a great movie from a bad script, or at least, very good movie from a very stupid idea, my vote is John Woo’s FACE/OFF. 
7. Film is a visual language, not a literary one. The script for The Revenant is bare bones and mediocre at best, but Alejandro's vision and the actor's performances make that film a truly spectacular one. Plus, Lubezki's camera is incredible. 

## 2. The most effective "Low Budget" world-building you’ve ever seen?

**Score:** 29
**Body:** I recently rewatched Coherence (2013) and was floored by how much tension and "scale" they managed to build within a single house, using almost no VFX. It made me realize that massive CGI budgets often feel "smaller" than a well-written, contained mystery. When a film relies on dialogue and subtle environmental cues to imply a larger world, rather than showing us a $200 million cityscape, the audience’s imagination does the heavy lifting, often creating a more visceral experience.

​In Coherence, the world-building is strictly internal and psychological. We learn about the physics of the comet and the branching realities through the characters' frantic theories and a few simple props (the glow sticks, the box). This "information-based" world-building feels more grounded than the lore-heavy exposition we see in modern blockbusters.

​Another great example is The Man from Earth, which takes place entirely in a living room but spans 14,000 years of history just through the power of storytelling. Similarly, Primer manages to make a suburban garage feel like the epicenter of a complex, terrifying scientific breakthrough without a single flashy explosion.

​I’m looking for more examples of "Information-Dense" world-building. What are some films that managed to create a massive sense of scale or a complex universe while clearly working with a shoestring budget? Are there recent or more obscure examples that you feel use their limitations as a creative strength?

​I’m particularly interested in movies where the "world" is built through sound design, dialogue, or clever editing rather than physical sets. How do these films maintain their immersion without the visual crutch of high-end production design?

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. For starters, *Stalker*. Never a film managed to create such an otherwordly dimension through the coalesce of the power of poetic language and wasteland-like locations.

*Stalker* slowly develops an unsettling sense of abandonment. However, the viewer (just as the three main characters) is irrationally driven to simply go forward, in order to find the so-called *zone*. Spaced-out set of rituals and inner human turmoil encompass brooding, post-industrial sounds. Suddenly, *the Zone* feels like some sort of unearthed, new faith.

This enraptured experience finally pays off. Things are revealed. Unthinkable images come out of nowhere. The world suddenly changed, but, then again, nothing really changed. Yet the mutual feeling of having touched the other side of reality is now transferred to the viewer. You were there, too.
  - Too bad the wasteland-like locations actually had poisonous waste :( In a sense Tarkovsky and other crew members paid a high cost.
2. Primer was made for I believe $3,000. There is a lot going on that we don’t fully see and has to be inferred. 

Angel’s Egg clearly has a whole lot going on in its world but you won’t ever get answers. 
  - what happened after that part?
3. Fritz Lang Metropolis. i know they had quite the budget for a silent era film, probably around 20 million in modern USD, but even today i still have no idea how they pulled that off with 20s tech. Considering how rudimentary the materials available were, it’s remarkable that modern filmmakers come back to it over and over as inspiration 
4. Monsters (2010) was famous for that. Just a couple scenes with some glowing species glued to a tree, but the majority is just shot guerilla style in latin america and the information is mainly relayed through how people talk about the zone, as you say.



Another one comes to mind, propably because of recency, is bugonia. That one had real hollywood budget, but from the techniques employed it actually comes close to what you are asking. You could see a talented indie crew pulling that one off on a shoestring budget, coming very close to the result.



Then pretty similar to Bugonia, actually imho much better even, and actual mini budget Brit Marlings early *Sound of my Voice* is fantastic and very in line with the movies you mention. You really get that sense of the fantastic, all just through the writing and her great acting. I think that one maybe comes closest to utilizing exactly what you are looking for: You can see clearly the great irony of it working so well, being so intoxicating exactly by not employing weird effects. The mini budget and the sense of the fantastical play into each other. It kind of plays into how we know these kind of guru-figures in our own lifes, but then what if we met someone like that who then just kept surprising us, as if she was actually what she said she was, and it kept holding up to even a very sceptical high iq person. The mundanity of the setting plays into the notion of the fantastic, it feels closer to something that could happen to ourselves.
5. Dialogue can do a lot of heavy lifting when it comes to worldbuilding.

Probably the best example, a single line from Star Wars, "You fought in the Clone Wars?"

That one line of dialogue sets up so much. There was a recent and impactful conflict that's somehow tied to the current state of things (i.e. the Empire being in power), it involved clones which says a lot about the state of technology for this world (and for years before the prequels, it was stated that the clones were evil clones of Jedi that served the "Clone Masters," so it really shows the range of possibilities this one line can set up), and Jedi like Obi-Wan were involved in this war.

## 3. Winnie the Pooh and the Honey Tree (1966)

**Score:** 22
**Body:** Pooh's odyssey is far more compelling than Odysseus's; is the chimerical search for an endless source of hunny not the greatest question posed to man since the meaning of life?

I will say that the sometimes controversial inclusion of the American workman, Gopher, does work in my eyes, because only he could make the rookie mistake of handing an already bloated, wedged Pooh a tub of more of his choice succour.

This animated short is a hermetically sealed philtre of priceless nostalgia chronicling that aforementioned expotition in ways that will never fail to inextricably endear the Silly Old Bear to us. Pooh is as cute as ever, his tumbly as rumbly as usual, and the animation remains a sure source of dopamine (especially because Eeyore's black trials and tribulations are left unnoticed and unaddressed...again).

P. S. Pooh will be taking one bucket of hunny (many more if it's no bother; think about it) from each of you at the door; it is no mean feat being a little black cloud filled to the gullet with busy bees for the sake of our amusement!

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This film is strongest when it pays respect to the original novels by A. A. Milne, and the illustrations by E. H. Shepard. There are departures to be sure (an American character in this story is confused and anachronistic) but this Pooh accurately captures the British bourgeois ideal that is the core of the character. He achieves wisdom through kindness, self-actualization through leisure, and security through community. Much of this was lost in subsequent sequels and adaptation, as modern American capitalist ideals got in the way of who Pooh is and what he means to all of us.
  - What a truly fantastic comment. Your lines about what he achieved, especially "self-actualisation through leisure" and "security through community", are genuinely, despite this only being about little old Pooh bear, some of the pithiest and most succinct analyses I have read about a character—especially given the want for literacy and thought that exists today.

I find Gopher to be a funny intrusion; he never spends enough time on screen to really hog any time, and when he does have a line or two, I find myself enjoying the absurdity of his placement. 

I agree with you wholeheartedly about the more recent adaptations too; I spent some time last year watching as much Pooh on film as I could, and I found that the latter films are utterly devoid of a truly British, rustic sensibility when compared to the earlier productions. More than that, they seem to exist purely to appease the American child's proclivities with regard to the media they choose to watch and to keep them comfortable in the process of it. The stakes of each story are lower, and, unlike most of the earlier films and a few exceptions, the danger never feels truly precarious or serious.
    - All jokes aside, though, I did unabashedly love Christopher Robin (2018). You can nitpick the plot, but that movie *nails* Pooh and is jam-packed with whimsy. It’s also hilarious and incredibly cute.
      - Oh, me too. Without a doubt. That film was one of my favourites. Indeed, so much uncontrolled whimsy, and it really captured the sullen spirit that sometimes pervades the beginning or middle of Pooh and his collective's stories, which then evaporates away by the end as they come together and achieve something greater than their individual strife. A truly worthy live-action adaptation and the jokes, puns, and bits they wrote for it are fucking incredible. 
  - yeah I feel like OP is trolling but I genuinely consider Milne's works to be masterpieces of children's literature, and Shepard's illustrations amazing as well.
    - Have you read any of my responses? It would be very odd to conclude I am trolling if you have. The original post was obviously a little tongue-in-cheek, but I do very much hold Pooh in high regard. 
2. For anybody planning to comment—there have been three comments so far and unfortunately none of them have appeared because of the character minimum! 180 characters minimum, everybody! Annoying, but mandated.

## 4. Terence Davies's 'The Deep Blue Sea' (2011) Reviewed

**Score:** 5
**Body:** A quip about a cubist painting at an art gallery does not land; Rachel Weisz's uncertain Hester asks Tom Hiddleston's childish Freddie ''where are you going?!'' after a representative bout between them (short, frequent, public, and loud) because of said joke failing; he howls back, ''to the impressionists!''. This story unfurls itself with both movements in a medley—the multiperspectival, structured approach of Cubism and the short brushstrokes of Impressionistic art, fleeting scenes captured with a thick layer of emotional representation and without a clear sense of chronology.

Terence Davies's film 'The Deep Blue Sea', an adaptation of the play with the self-same title, is a work of elegiac proportions; it deals with its subject matter of tragic romantic entanglement in a flux of flashes that are sometimes abstract and other times more grounded. Weisz's performance, for me, garners sympathy because of her disquieted wandering; her inability to decide and move on from her infatuation in an age when women had their lives decided for them is not at all something to object to, at least as an understandable predicament. Whether or not her personal character is of merit or not is a different matter entirely and, I suppose, one that is judged more subjectively. But her present situation as the film begins is exceedingly sympathetic.

If we are being frank, former RAF pilot (though you wouldn't know it given how relentlessly he talks of those immodest days as if they were yesterday), Freddie is a cad and a bounder. At least to me, his callousness towards Hester is unconscionable. This can be explained away with the implications of his war-torn brain, but it does not excuse much. We do not necessarily see the extent of vexation that Hester spins according to Page's accusations, but his incongruous aggression towards her during his alcoholic rage is incredible regardless of said shortcomings, if they exist at all to that degree. I say it is incongruous because of the reserve of the age and, more specifically, his physical manifestation as a terribly soigné man; his furores do not fit him as well as his mid-century suits do.


Following this, Hester becomes an eminently understandable person, and Weisz's acting is more often subtle than not, composed of devastating, flitting micro-expressions and syllables formed with simmering pathos and said in understated fashion. It is a performance that maintains the formula of Davies's film very well: a film composed of a hazy, soft resolution that manages to melt the icy scenarios and dialogue which are almost always taking place in the story. Another performance worth mentioning is Ann Mitchell's as Mrs Elton, Hester's landlady after her separation from her husband. Mrs Elton is an expert in the field of distaff wisdom for the mistreated, as Hester is, which can only be delivered by a woman with as much avuncular warmth as her. Mitchell immediately subdues the viewer from assuming any conniving intentions in her with an inexplicably good-natured countenance.

Thematically, 'The Deep Blue Sea' embodies a great wound. One that has raged and bled away already and is now deciding whether it will reform itself, like Hester after Freddie leaves her for good and suicide is no longer appealing. Like Freddie after he leaves Hester for a test pilot job in South America to relive his halcyon days in the RAF. Like Sir William Collyer once he finally grants a divorce to Hester after she unceremoniously left him. And most of all, like rubble-ridden Britain in 1950, a country full of people who fought and clamoured for peace but are now left to do that again in their own, tenuous personal lives. It is nigh on impossible to not feel sympathy for everybody involved; so soon after a genuinely existential match nobody should be made to meet—war.

More than anything else in this film, it is Davies's customary cinematic decisions that charmed me. The plunging, weighty silences between lines; the unmistakably Terence Davies opening ten minutes or so, where the story comes to an excellently graceful and equally tender arrangement of bodies; how the film, thanks to Florian Hoffmeister's camera, looks like a memory slowly fading away from recall and simultaneously feels like a smudged postcard; his decision to use Samuel Barber's timeless violin concerto to beautifully score these bleak scenes whilst generating a sense of urgency; and the brevity of it all... The flashback sequence at the underground station during the Blitz, especially with communal song and the spirit of perseverance, is just an utterly unparalleled example of Terence Davies's gift of capturing ordinary people in either extraordinary or quotidian circumstances without warping our resonance to those disparate kinds of moments into unfamiliarity, even if they are fundamentally unfamiliar; his devising of scenes always tapped into communicable humanity first and foremost.

I always feel, with Davies more than the vast majority of filmmakers, the actual life of the artist in the rhythm of their films. This is felt once again with 'The Deep Blue Sea'; despite the story's continual rebirth and reinterpretation as a play, his distinct voice of emotionality and sentimental longing impresses deeply upon us in this film version. Terence Davies is a stalwart of British directing, and, thankfully, he developed a truly idiosyncratic mind and vision for it—one that lives on in his wake and will continue to do so as long as cinematic power is venerated.

## 5. Musings on Close Your Eyes...

**Score:** 2
**Body:** Not really any words. It's not easy to get affected by a film, but it did in words I can't really describe. It's not often that I'll sit after a film and just take it in for a bit. It's not often that a film could do that, if ever? Maybe the only other one was Erice too, El Sur. Is Erice one of the few directors that can grasp that magic, that indescribable magic that can flower from film?

It's sad in a way. Because you know that you won't see many films like it. Films like that today too? Perhaps you'd get more back in the golden days but even then, with all the cinema I've seen, it doesn't really come close to it. You can watch films, and a lot of them will be good, but when you watch something like that you kind of know that it has a gulf between them, a distant veil. Though instead of lingering in that we should probably celebrate what we get, that we could even get a film like it. 

Describing it doesn't really do anything, doesn't really get it. It takes the full nearly 3 hours to get to it. It's something that's seen, heard, felt. Felt being the thing. It's history of cinema backwards and forwards. It's the luminousity of art, art coming together into film. Words fade away and we are left with an image. Images that we see and that are on the screen. We're left with faces, faces and our own. 

It's not an easy film to recommend, who would like it? Maybe all I could say is it's for the dreamers, it's for feelers, it's for magic. All I can say is that the film affected me in ways I can't explain, but I can say that by the end, you believe in film, cinema, art
